# Transcrição: 04-How to Value Your Acquisition Target

- **Fonte:** 04-How to Value Your Acquisition Target
- **Duração:** 11:30

## [00:00 - 03:03]

Hi, and welcome back. In this module, I'm going to go over everything you need to know about the business valuation process. This is when we take the financials you've learned so much about in the previous two modules and use them to establish a market value of the business. Sophisticated buyers base their valuations on objective analysis in three key areas. Predictability and potential. Now I just want to reiterate that if you don't have a professional, this is the time to bring one in. Analyzing the performance of the business, the predictability of the future, and the overall potential in the market takes a level of sophistication that you should be hiring. This will set the value of the business that you will then use to justify your offer to the current owners. It's important this part of the process is done absolutely right. I'm going to walk you through this entire process using examples. At the end of this module, you should feel confident in driving the valuation process for yourself. So let's get started. I like to take a three prong approach to valuing a business. I look at assets, markets, and incomes. When evaluating a business's assets, I like to examine the cost required to build the existing asset and replace costs if those assets were to be lost. This might include machinery, equipment, vehicles, buildings, etc. You can easily establish a base value for these assets with a simple equation. Assets minus outstanding liabilities equals base asset value. You're establishing what the value of all the assets minus any outstanding liabilities such as loans or other forms of debt against those assets. But this value is only one part of my holistic approach to establishing the overall value of the business I want to purchase. Next, you must examine the company's position in the market. The easiest way to do this is by performing a comparable company analysis or CCA for short. A comparable company analysis allows you to compare the business to competing public companies and other purchase transactions of similar industry size. This is where your experts are really going to come in helpful. I promise you this. This analysis operates under the assumption that similar companies in the same industry have similar values. There are some online databases you can use to do this research yourself. But as I said earlier, I'd hire a professional valuation expert and they can do this for you. Once this research is done, you should be able to establish average multiples for the group of comparable companies that you're looking at. Multiples is the number by which you'll multiply the company's EBITDA to establish its market value, earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization.

## [03:03 - 06:04]

The formula looks like this. Market value equals multiple times adjusted EBITDA. I'll walk you through a simple example of this calculation in a minute. But let's talk about EBITDA. EBITDA is an acronym that stands for Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization. Simply put, EBITDA is a measure of profitability. It's more precise than net income because it shows earnings before the influence of accounting and financial deductions. We've talked a lot about leveraged buyouts in this course so far. And now it's going to come full circle. EBITDA first came to prominence in the financial world around the 1980s by LBO investment bankers. They promoted it as a tool to determine whether a company could service its debt in the short term. Today, it's widely used to compare companies against each other and establish industry averages. I'll show you a quicker way to establish your target company's EBITDA. Here is a sample income statement we used in our previous lesson. First, you want to find the operating income. This is called EBIT, Earnings Before Interest and Taxes. Then, we add back any listed depreciation, which is the amount that the business has written down its assets by during the year. This should be included in the income statement as well. Then we add back in amortization. This is exactly the same as depreciation, but it's for assets that are intangible. You can't touch them, so intangible assets would include things like patents or good wills. Small businesses don't have amortization. And now you've arrived at your baseline EBITDA. But we want to go one step further and adjust the EBITDA for any non-recurring expenses you might have noticed in the income statement. This might include a large equipment or real estate purchase that is unlikely to occur every year. You want to add this back in to establish an even more accurate picture of the company's financial situation on any particular average year. And finally, you might also want to reduce this number by any estimated salary you might pay a manager to help you operate the business going forward. This is especially important if the current owner will be exiting the business entirely and is also the main day-to-day operator. Here is how that might look based on our sample income statement. Now that you've established an adjusted EBITDA, and thanks to your comparative analysis, you have an average multiple for the company similar to your target. These two numbers multiplied by one another equals your estimated market value. There are also some free and easy to use online calculators that help you establish EBITDA as well as the final market value. For example, on 10X by sell, you can do it online. On your screen below, we'll include the URL for our 10X by sell so you can do just that.

## [06:04 - 09:09]

This does not mean that's exactly what you should or would pay by the way for the company. You also need to consider the company's income. This is the third and final step in my valuation process. We need to establish an estimate of future cash flow. And the easiest way to do that is to perform a discounted cash flow analysis. The equation looks like this. I know that this looks complicated and quite honestly, it is unbelievably complicated. This is why this is another example of why you need a professional who knows how to simply do this kind of work. But if you want to figure it out yourself, we've included it in our example so you can learn to do it. The purpose of this analysis is to estimate the money you as an owner would receive from the business, adjusted for the time value of money. The time value of money assumes that a dollar today is worth more than a dollar tomorrow because it can be invested. Here is a very simple example of this concept. Assuming a 5% annual interest rate, $1 in a savings account would be worth $1.05 in a year. Similarly, if a $1 payment is delayed for a year, its present value is $0.95 because you cannot transfer it into your savings account to earn interest. To conduct a discounted cash flow analysis, an investor must make estimates about future cash flows and the ending value of the investment, equipment, or other assets. The investor must also determine an appropriate discount rate to use for this model. This is very important to constructing the financial aspect of the business. These analysis cannot be done without financial records that are accurate. Again, I can't stress enough the importance of having a true professional help you with this process. In the end, you'll be left with a prediction of a business's future cash flow, which will establish a future value. And if the predicted future cash flow of a business is above the price you pay for the business, well, then obviously that's a good investment. So you can see why it's important to approach valuing a company holistically by looking at the value of all assets, a comparison of competitors in the market, and the future cash flow potential of the business. But there are a few more considerations that you should take into account before you make your final offer. How much value can you put into your business and how big can you make it? How hard will it be to grow your business beyond where it's at? The risks associated with the different due diligence of the number of customers and the products and services it's offering, all these things, the number of competitors you have in the market, the number of key dependent employees you'll be dependent on that might leave. All these need to be taken into consideration before you establish your final multiple of

## [09:09 - 11:30]

EBITDA that you offer for this business. You also need to sit and think hard about any reasons a business should go for a premium or a deduction in price. There are always reasons to justify the business valuation, so let me give you some examples. Qualifications for a valuation premium. The company has specific barriers to entry that make it difficult or unlikely additional competitors will enter the market. Historically, and consistent higher margins and higher returns in competitors. Proprietary technology or patents that are difficult to replicate essentially does a business have a competitive advantage. Strategic fit with the buyer. Is there an opportunity to grow and scale and make it bigger or is it just something you really want and are you willing to pay up for that? And finally, provides access to new customers for the buyer, providing leverage and additional revenue opportunities for the buyer's existing business. So remember, these examples are reasons you'd pay up in value for business. Now let's talk about why you might discount the value of a business. Business is built heavily around the existing owner. A large percentage of the revenue is concentrated to a limited number of customers. Supply chain is limited to only a few suppliers. This could mean limited scalability and growth. Aging accounts receivable. Obsolete or rapidly depleting inventory. Expiring patents. Deferred maintenance or aging assets. Are there significant assets that you'll need to immediately replace and or repair that could cost a lot of money? New regulations that might limit profits in the future. ODE's, owner's discretionary earnings. If you have to replace the owner's contribution to the business with a bunch of employees, that will be a new cost so you can't add it to the profitability. If any of these things are true, you should consider increasing or decreasing your offer accordingly. And you should be able to justify that offer very easily in your offer letter. You'll learn everything about my process for developing an offer letter in the next two lessons from now. In the next module, I'll walk you through how to formally put together your offer. I'll see you there.
